claim: Green transition re-commoditization may reverse the secular decline in passthrough for specific input minerals (copper, lithium, rare earths) as electrification increases intensity of these materials.
needs: Empirical passthrough estimates for copper/lithium/rare earth minerals into consumer goods prices, which have never been estimated because these channels have never been material historically. IEA demand projections (50-70% copper growth by 2040) need to be connected to specific CPI passthrough mechanisms.

claim: 'Stacked' commodity shocks produce non-linearly worse inflation outcomes than the sum of individual shocks, because the first shock lowers the activation threshold for second-round channels.
needs: More than one clean observation. The 1979-80 shock arriving while 1973-74 institutional responses were active is essentially one data point with massive confounding factors. The 2021-23 parallel (COVID priming + Ukraine) is plausible but not yet formally analyzed. McKinsey repricing frequency data is suggestive but not causal.

claim: Active dedollarization is altering the FX/dollar channel mediation framework for commodity passthrough, with China settling $200B+ in yuan-denominated commodity trade in 2025.
needs: Data on whether dedollarization changes the passthrough mechanism or just the currency composition. Dollar share of commodity invoicing could stabilize at ~75%. The pace and ultimate magnitude remain deeply uncertain. Need to distinguish between settlement currency diversification and structural changes to the transmission mechanism.

claim: The credit impulse (second derivative of credit growth) is a superior operative variable to interest rates for predicting commodity-inflation demand outcomes, with a 1-2 quarter lead per the Biggs-Mayer-Pick framework.
needs: Specific calibration for commodity-driven episodes. BIS data showing credit impulse turning negative Q3 2022 with goods disinflation following in Q4 2022-Q1 2023 is consistent but not specifically calibrated. The leading indicator relationship is contested by mainstream macro. Needs cross-episode validation beyond the single 2022 observation.

claim: QT interacting with commodity-driven credit demand creates a compounding monetary squeeze that neither force alone would produce — central bank draining reserves while private sector requires credit expansion to finance higher-cost inventories.
needs: Empirical calibration of the interactive effect. The 2022 episode featured both QT and commodity shocks but disentangling their interactive effect from individual effects has not been attempted formally. Needs a formal model specifying the compounding mechanism and empirical tests separating additive from interactive effects.

claim: The BSL-to-private-credit contagion channel creates adverse selection and mark-to-market transmission during commodity stress, as best credits access cheaper BSL markets during benign conditions and worst credits are pushed to private credit during stress.
needs: Clarification of the ~30-40% BSL/private credit overlap figure (conflates stock and flow). Testing at current AUM levels ($1.7T private credit) — this channel has never been stress-tested. Private credit's defining feature (avoiding mark-to-market) partially insulates it from the described contagion, requiring a more nuanced mechanism specification.
